description: Solubility product constant, Ksp calculations, predicting precipitation, common ion effect, and factors affecting solubility.

problems.problem: Define solubility equilibrium. What is the solubility product constant ($K_{sp}$)?

step_by_step.explanation: Solubility equilibrium is the dynamic equilibrium between a solid solute and its dissolved ions in a saturated solution.
$K_{sp}$ is the equilibrium constant for the dissolution of a sparingly soluble ionic compound. It equals the product of the ion concentrations raised to their stoichiometric coefficients.

problems.final_answer: Solubility equilibrium: solid $\rightleftharpoons$ ions in saturated solution; $K_{sp}$ = product of ion concentrations at equilibrium

problems.problem: Write the $K_{sp}$ expression for the dissolution of calcium fluoride ($\text{CaF}_2$).
Write the $K_{sp}$ expression for silver chromate ($\text{Ag}_2\text{CrO}_4$).
The molar solubility of $\text{AgCl}$ is $1.3 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{M}$. Calculate the $K_{sp}$ of $\text{AgCl}$.
The molar solubility of $\text{CaF}_2$ is $2.1 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{M}$. Calculate the $K_{sp}$ of $\text{CaF}_2$.
The $K_{sp}$ of $\text{PbCl}_2$ is $1.7 \times 10^{-5}$. Calculate the molar solubility of $\text{PbCl}_2$.
The $K_{sp}$ of $\text{Ag}_2\text{CrO}_4$ is $1.1 \times 10^{-12}$. Calculate the molar solubility of $\text{Ag}_2\text{CrO}_4$.

For $\text{AgCl}$, $K_{sp} = 1.8 \times 10^{-10}$. Will a precipitate form when $50.0\ \text{mL}$ of $0.010\ \text{M}$ $\text{AgNO}_3$ is mixed with $50.0\ \text{mL}$ of $0.010\ \text{M}$ $\text{NaCl}$?

step_by_step.explanation: Since $Q_{sp} > K_{sp}$, a precipitate will form

problems.final_answer: Yes, a precipitate of $\text{AgCl}$ will form

problems.problem: For $\text{CaF}_2$, $K_{sp} = 3.2 \times 10^{-11}$. Will a precipitate form when $100.0\ \text{mL}$ of $0.0010\ \text{M}$ $\text{Ca(NO}_3)_2$ is mixed with $100.0\ \text{mL}$ of $0.0020\ \text{M}$ $\text{NaF}$?

step_by_step.explanation: Since $Q_{sp} > K_{sp}$, a precipitate will form

problems.final_answer: Yes, a precipitate of $\text{CaF}_2$ will form

problems.problem: Define the common ion effect. How does it affect the solubility of a salt?

step_by_step.explanation: The common ion effect is the decrease in solubility of an ionic compound when a soluble compound containing a common ion is added to the solution.
Adding a common ion shifts the equilibrium toward the solid (Le Châtelier's principle), reducing the concentration of dissolved ions and decreasing solubility.

problems.final_answer: Common ion effect = decreased solubility due to addition of an ion already present in the equilibrium

problems.problem: Calculate the solubility of $\text{AgCl}$ in a $0.010\ \text{M}$ $\text{NaCl}$ solution. ($K_{sp}$ of $\text{AgCl} = 1.8 \times 10^{-10}$)

step_by_step.step: Set up equilibrium with common ion
step_by_step.calculation: Let $s$ = solubility; $[\text{Ag}^+] = s$, $[\text{Cl}^-] = 0.010 + s \approx 0.010$ (since $s$ is small)

problems.problem: Calculate the solubility of $\text{CaF}_2$ in a $0.010\ \text{M}$ $\text{Ca(NO}_3)_2$ solution. ($K_{sp}$ of $\text{CaF}_2 = 3.2 \times 10^{-11}$)

step_by_step.step: Set up equilibrium with common ion

problems.problem: A solution contains $0.010\ \text{M}$ $\text{Ag}^+$ and $0.010\ \text{M}$ $\text{Pb}^{2+}$. Which will precipitate first when $\text{Cl}^-$ is added slowly? ($K_{sp}$ $\text{AgCl} = 1.8 \times 10^{-10}$, $K_{sp}$ $\text{PbCl}_2 = 1.7 \times 10^{-5}$)

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{AgCl}$ precipitates at much lower $[\text{Cl}^-]$ ($1.8 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{M}$) than $\text{PbCl}_2$ ($0.041\ \text{M}$)

problems.problem: The $K_{sp}$ of $\text{Mg(OH)}_2$ is $1.2 \times 10^{-11}$. Calculate the solubility of $\text{Mg(OH)}_2$ in pure water and in a solution with pH = 12.0.
problems.final_answer: Pure water: $1.44 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{M}$; at pH 12: $1.2 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{M}$ (decreased solubility due to common ion effect)

problems.problem: The $K_{sp}$ of $\text{Fe(OH)}_2$ is $4.9 \times 10^{-17}$. Calculate the solubility of $\text{Fe(OH)}_2$ at pH 8.0.

step_by_step.step: Set up equilibrium with common ion

problems.problem: The $K_{sp}$ of $\text{AgCl}$ is $1.8 \times 10^{-10}$. What is the solubility of $\text{AgCl}$ in $0.10\ \text{M}$ $\text{NH}_3$? (Assume $\text{Ag}^+$ forms complex $\text{Ag(NH}_3)_2^+$ with $K_f = 1.6 \times 10^7$)
Explain how pH affects the solubility of a salt containing a basic anion (e.g., $\text{OH}^-$, $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$, $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$).

step_by_step.explanation: Adding acid (lowering pH) consumes the basic anion ($\text{OH}^-$, $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$, etc.) through protonation.
Removing the anion shifts the solubility equilibrium toward the dissolved ions, increasing solubility.

step_by_step.calculation: $\text{CaCO}_3(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{Ca}^{2+}(aq) + \text{CO}_3^{2-}(aq)$; adding $\text{H}^+$ forms $\text{HCO}_3^-$ and $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$, removing $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$, increasing solubility.

problems.final_answer: Lower pH (more acidic) increases solubility of salts with basic anions by consuming the anion and shifting equilibrium toward dissolution

problems.problem: The $K_{sp}$ of $\text{AgBr}$ is $5.0 \times 10^{-13}$. Will a precipitate form when $0.010\ \text{M}$ $\text{AgNO}_3$ is added to $0.10\ \text{M}$ $\text{KBr}$? Calculate $Q_{sp}$.

step_by_step.explanation: If adding $\text{AgNO}_3$ directly to $\text{KBr}$ solution, $[\text{Ag}^+] = 0.010\ \text{M}$, $[\text{Br}^-] = 0.10\ \text{M}$
Since $Q_{sp} > K_{sp}$, precipitate will form

problems.problem: The $K_{sp}$ of $\text{BaSO}_4$ is $1.1 \times 10^{-10}$. Calculate the solubility of $\text{BaSO}_4$ in $0.10\ \text{M}$ $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$.

step_by_step.step: Set up equilibrium with common ion

problems.problem: A solution contains $0.010\ \text{M}$ $\text{Cl}^-$ and $0.010\ \text{M}$ $\text{CrO}_4^{2-}$. When $\text{Ag}^+$ is added slowly, which precipitates first? ($K_{sp}$ $\text{AgCl} = 1.8 \times 10^{-10}$, $K_{sp}$ $\text{Ag}_2\text{CrO}_4 = 1.1 \times 10^{-12}$)

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{AgCl}$ precipitates at much lower $[\text{Ag}^+]$ ($1.8 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{M}$) than $\text{Ag}_2\text{CrO}_4$ ($1.05 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{M}$)

problems.problem: The dissolution of $\text{PbCl}_2(s)$ is endothermic ($\Delta H > 0$). Predict how $K_{sp}$ and solubility change when temperature increases.

step_by_step.explanation: Endothermic dissolution: $\text{PbCl}_2(s) + \text{heat} \rightleftharpoons \text{Pb}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{Cl}^-(aq)$
Increasing temperature adds heat, shifting equilibrium right toward dissolved ions
More ions in solution means both $K_{sp}$ and solubility increase

problems.final_answer: Both $K_{sp}$ and solubility increase with temperature (endothermic dissolution)

problems.problem: A solution contains $0.010\ \text{M}$ $\text{Cl}^-$ and $0.010\ \text{M}$ $\text{CrO}_4^{2-}$. When $\text{Ag}^+$ is added, $\text{AgCl}$ precipitates first. What is $[\text{Cl}^-]$ when $\text{Ag}_2\text{CrO}_4$ just begins to precipitate? ($K_{sp}$ $\text{AgCl} = 1.8 \times 10^{-10}$, $K_{sp}$ $\text{Ag}_2\text{CrO}_4 = 1.1 \times 10^{-12}$)
The solubility of $\text{CaSO}_4$ is $0.67\ \text{g/L}$. Calculate $K_{sp}$. (Molar mass $\text{CaSO}_4 = 136.14\ \text{g/mol}$)
Kidney stones often contain calcium oxalate ($\text{CaC}_2\text{O}_4$, $K_{sp} = 2.3 \times 10^{-9}$). Explain why drinking more water helps prevent kidney stone formation.

step_by_step.explanation: Drinking water increases urine volume, decreasing $[\text{Ca}^{2+}]$ and $[\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}]$
Lower ion concentrations reduce $Q_{sp}$, keeping $Q_{sp} < K_{sp}$ and preventing precipitation

problems.final_answer: Dilution decreases ion concentrations, keeping $Q_{sp} < K_{sp}$ and preventing $\text{CaC}_2\text{O}_4$ precipitation

problems.problem: Explain why $\text{Al(OH)}_3$ is more soluble at both very low pH and very high pH compared to neutral pH.

step_by_step.explanation: At low pH, $\text{H}^+$ consumes $\text{OH}^-$, shifting $\text{Al(OH)}_3(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{Al}^{3+} + 3\text{OH}^-$ right
At high pH, excess $\text{OH}^-$ forms soluble complex ion $\text{Al(OH)}_4^-$
$\text{Al(OH)}_3$ is amphoteric: dissolves in both acid (as $\text{Al}^{3+}$) and base (as $\text{Al(OH)}_4^-$)

problems.final_answer: Amphoteric: dissolves in acid (consumes $\text{OH}^-$) and base (forms $\text{Al(OH)}_4^-$ complex)

problems.problem: Which is more soluble: $\text{AgCl}$ ($K_{sp} = 1.8 \times 10^{-10}$) or $\text{Ag}_2\text{CrO}_4$ ($K_{sp} = 1.1 \times 10^{-12}$)? Calculate molar solubility of each.

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{Ag}_2\text{CrO}_4$ has lower $K_{sp}$ but higher molar solubility (different stoichiometry)
